motions.infoslide: Self-directed learning describes a process in which individuals take the initiative, with or without the help of others, in diagnosing their learning needs.

motions.motion: This house believes that we should allow businesses to fire employees on the basis of personal (legal) online behavior (e.g. personal Facebook posts).
motions.infoslide: Online behavior refers to the actions, conduct, and interactions that individuals engage in while using the internet or participating in online platforms and ocmmunities. It encompasses how individuals communicate, express themselves, and engage with tohers in the digital realms. Online behavior can include activities such as positng and sharing content, commenting on posts, sending messages, participating in online discussions, and engaging in online gaming or virtual environments.

motions.motion: THBT children should be placed in classes based on academic ability instead of age
motions.infoslide: In most countries, children in primary and secondary school are sorted into classes based on their age. 'Academic ability' refers to the knowledge of the students, theira cademic results and level of engagement with the material being taught.

motions.motion: THW choose a career based off of money, instead of passion
motions.infoslide: A career refers to an individual's professional journey or the sequence of jobs, roles, and experiences they undertake throughout their working life. A career typically involves a long-term commitment to developing skills, acquiring knowledge, and achieving personal and professional growth.

motions.motion: THW ban all anonymopus accounts on scoial media sites
motions.infoslide: An anonymous accoutn on social media refers to a user profile that conceals the true identity of the person behind it. It allows individuals to participate in online platforms without revealing their real name, personal information, or recognizable details. Instead, they may use pseudonyms or avatars to maintain their anonymity.

motions.motion: THS heritable human genome editing
motions.infoslide: Heritable human genome editing (HGE) allows scinetists to make changes in human DNA, the material that carries information about how humans will look and functionm that can be passed to future generations.

motions.motion: THBT all children should raise a pet
motions.infoslide: A pet is an animal that is domesticated or kept for companionship, pleasure, or amusement. Pets are typically kept in households and treated as members of the family. They are often chosen based on their ability to provide emotional support, companionship, and entertainment to their owners. Common types of pets include dogs, cats, birds, fish, rabbits, hamsters, guinea pigs, and reptiles, among others. Pets require care and attention, including feeding, grooming, exercise, and medical attention, to ensure their well-being and happiness.

motions.motion: THW allow individuals in prison to serve their sentences in the armed forces
motions.infoslide: The motion refers to a system where incarcerated individuals have the opportunity to join the military and fulfil their sentences while simultaneously undergoing military training and sercing in military roles.

motions.motion: THBT children's books featuring characters from racial minorities should prioritize stories that do not focus on racial injustice
motions.infoslide: Racial injustice refers to the unfair or discriminatory treatment of individuals or groups based on their race or ethnicity

motions.motion: That war journalists should be forced to reveal their sources in international criminal tribunals (including the International Criminal Court) when trying cases of war crimes, crimes against humanity or genocide
That the environment movement should heavily prioritise nuclear power in their energy advocacy
That we oppose the use of facial recognition technology by supermarkets.
motions.infoslide: Facial recognition technology is being trialled by supermarkets to try reduce crime by attempting to match, in real time, the faces of people who enter a store against that store’s record of offenders and accomplices.

motions.motion: That Indonesia should legalise the sale of votes to political parties
That students from financially disadvantaged backgrounds should be given a lower bar to qualify for academic special considerations
motions.infoslide: Special considerations include, but are not limited to, having a longer time to submit assignments and applications, and having an easier pathway to passing university courses and degrees

motions.motion: This House would deduct points from sporting teams when their players are found guilty of racism or homophobia.
This House would allow pharmaceutical companies to advertise their products to consumers
motions.infoslide: In the USA and New Zealand, pharmaceutical companies are allow to advertise medicines.

motions.motion: This House regrets the existence of Valentine’s day.
motions.infoslide: Valentine’s Day is celebrated on February 14, when couples express their affection with greetings and gifts.

motions.motion: This House would limit the copyright period for media to five years.
motions.infoslide: Creators of media (like films, books, TV) can hold the copyright. This means that no one else is allowed to make media with those ideas. In some countries, the copyright lasts over 100 years.

motions.motion: This House believes that the government should charge higher taxes on goods and services with a big carbon footprint.
motions.infoslide: A carbon footprint is how much carbon something uses. A bigger footprint uses more carbon and is worse for the environment.

motions.motion: This house, as a voter in a democratic country, would prioritise individual candidate qualities over party affiliation when voting in elections
motions.infoslide: Party affiliation refers to the political party or group a candidate belongs to. On the other hand, individual candidate qualities refer to traits that the candidate possesses, including but not limited to experience, positions on key issues, and personal beliefs.

motions.motion: This house regrets the dominant belief that humility is a virtue
motions.infoslide: Humility is defined as the quality of being modest and having a low view of one’s importance.

motions.motion: This house would impose a BBC-style impartiality requirement on all news platforms
motions.infoslide: The BBC (British Broadcasting Corporation) is legally required to report news impartially. This means it should not choose sides on controversial topics, and the amount of time given to each opinion should roughly match how many people support that opinion in society. However, the BBC does not have to stay neutral on fundamental state issues, like on issues regarding the rule of law and the fairness of the democratic process.

motions.motion: This house would ban the use of artificial intelligence systems in judicial decision-making processes
motions.infoslide: Currently, AI systems are being introduced experimentally in judicial systems in various jurisdictions, e.g. predictive algorithms to suggest sentencing or bail outcomes.

motions.motion: This house believes that schools in developing countries should priotise a specialised education system over a liberal education system.
motions.infoslide: A specialised education system involves students studying fewer subjects selected according to their interests from an early stage in their schooling, focussing on depth in those specific areas. A liberal education system involves students studying a diverse array of subjects designed to develop general knowledge, critical thinking, and interdisciplinary understanding.

motions.motion: This house believes that the feminist movement should prioritise the advocacy of structural reform, rather than challenging social norms
motions.infoslide: Structural reform refers to policy-level institutional changes (e.g. creating laws) aimed at improving people's lives. Examples include pushing for subsidized childcare and anti-discrimination laws. On the other hand, challenging social norms refers to efforts to shift societal attitudes around gender. Examples include questioning beauty standards, toxic masculinity and gender roles.

motions.motion: Thise house believes that the feminist movement should prioritise the advocacy of structural reform, rather than challenging social norms
motions.infoslide: Structural reform refers to policy-level institutional changes (e.g. creating laws) aimed at improving people's lives. Examples include pushing for subsidized childcare and anti-discrimination laws. On the other hand, challenging social norms refers to efforts to shift societal attitudes around gender. Examples include questioning beauty standards, toxic masculinity and gender roles.

motions.motion: This house supports religions characterizing human nature as innately evil (e.g., the original sin in Christianity)
motions.infoslide: Human nature refers to the characteristics, feelings, and behaviors that are considered typical or innate to humans. It includes qualities that are believed to be part of being human across cultures and time.

motions.motion: This house believes that governments should heavily invest in geo-engineering to combat global warming
motions.infoslide: Geo-engineering refers to large-scale technologies used to purposely change Earth's climate. Examples include spraying tiny reflective particles high in the atmosphere to bounce sunlight back into space, altering ocean surfaces to absorb more carbon dioxide, and covering large areas of land with reflective materials to reflect more sunlight instead of letting it warm the Earth.

motions.motion: This house would ban the use of cars.
motions.infoslide: In recent years, countries have begun a move towards increasing public transport services due to the environmental concerns around cars and the high costs of building roads and highways to accommodate for cars. Public transport includes buses, trains, and any form of transportation meant to transport large groups of people. In response, many car companies have begun producing electric vehicles, which are more environmentally friendly.

motions.motion: This house believes that parents should not promote university education as the primary path to success in life
motions.infoslide: University education is often viewed as a standard route to career stability, higher income, and social status. However, alternative paths such as vocational training, entrepreneurship, and creative industries also lead to successful and fulfilling lives.

motions.motion: This house regrets the existence of parenting influencers
motions.infoslide: Parenting influencers are individuals who upload videos or stream on platforms like YouTube or Twitch. With the primary subject of their videos being about how they raise their kids, videos about their life as a parent, and content advising other parents on how they should be raising their children.

motions.motion: This house would ban fast fashion
motions.infoslide: Fast fashion refers to quickly made, inexpensive clothing that follows the latest trends and is produced in large quantities. Brands like Zara, H&M, and Shein are known for fast fashion, offering new styles every few weeks at low prices.

motions.motion: This house would ban all fast food restaurants (e.g., McDonald's, KFC, Domino's, Taco Bell, etc.)
motions.infoslide: Fast food restaurants run on a model of making cheap and unhealthy food, that is accessible to many people but is also bad to consume in the long term. These restaurants have been linked with increasing obesity, heart problems, and nutritional deficiencies. But also, they provide a lot of part time work for individuals and have become a significant part of many local economies.

motions.motion: This house believes that team sports are better than individual sports
motions.infoslide: Team sports like volleyball, basketball, and soccer, prioritise the success of the team as a whole. Where teamwork is what allows for players to win and any joy from success is shared across the team. Individual sports like badminton, tennis, and boxing, prioritise the success of the individual. Every win is due to the player's own efforts and is proof that they are the best in their respective sport.

motions.motion: This house believes that the feminist movement should oppose popular fairy tales which portray traditional gender roles.
motions.infoslide: Many classic fairy tales, such as Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, and Snow White, portray female protagonists as passive, gentle, and dependent on external rescue—often by a male hero. These characters are frequently valued for their beauty, obedience, and kindness rather than their agency or decision-making. In contrast, male characters like princes are typically cast as brave, active saviors who resolve the central conflict and determine the story's outcome, reinforcing traditional gender expectations of women as caregivers and men as protectors.

motions.motion: This house believes that aid agencies should not be allowed to undertake missionary activities.
motions.infoslide: Aid agencies are organizations that provide humanitarian assistance, such as food, healthcare, and disaster relief, often in response to crises or long- term development needs. Missionary activities are efforts, usually led by religious groups, to spread their faith while often providing social services like education, medical care, and community support.

motions.motion: This house believes that the development of AI will do more harm than good to education.
motions.infoslide: Artificial intelligence (AI), has risen in popularity in recent years with many teachers using it to write exams, create worksheets and help them mark homework. Students have also begun to start using AI to improve their own understanding of subjects but also to cheat on exams or to have AI do their homework for them. Because of AI's improving ability to write like a human, it is becoming increasingly difficult to identify AI works. Some people are concerned that this increase in AI usage may limit the amount that students are actually learning, whilst others believe that AI will make teaching easier and allow students to ask more questions and get help with their studies even when their teachers aren't around.

motions.motion: This house believes that environmental movements should heavily prioritise geopoliticised narratives to advocate for greener technologies and policies
motions.infoslide: "Geopoliticisation", in the context of energy and climate issues, refers to narratives that justify policy decisions through a geopolitical lens (e.g., advocating for decreasing the reliance on fossil fuels in the EU to reduce dependence on Russia, promoting domestically manufacturing electric vehicles in the US to compete with other producers etc.)

motions.motion: This house would raise their child to prefer being single
motions.infoslide: Some people seek out romantic relationships and consider them an important life goal and a source of fulfillment, while others believe that staying single is the preferable or "default" lifestyle. Such preferences, among other factors, are modelled by parents through advice, guidance, encouragement and role modelling.

motions.motion: This house believes that news organisations should prioritise “slow journalism” over real-time reporting
motions.infoslide: Slow journalism is a news model that prioritises depth, verification, and long-form investigation over rapid, real-time updates. It produces fewer, more thoroughly researched stories, avoiding breaking-news style reporting.

motions.motion: This house believes that corporations should adopt the practice of "radical transparency"
motions.infoslide: Certain companies operate on the practice of Radical Transparency. This means that internally every conversation, meeting, and report is recorded and available to all employees upon their request. Additionally, employees are required to rank each interaction and collaboration with one another, effectively reviewing each other.

motions.motion: This house opposes the rise of health-based influencing
motions.infoslide: In recent years, influencers have increasingly begun producing physical and mental health-based content. Examples include dietary recommendations, the marketing of certain products, general lifestyle advice (e.g., "gymfluencers" promoting exercise routines), and sharing techniques which are supposed to aid in reducing stress and improving mental health

motions.motion: This house believes that the rise of Artificial Intelligence will increase, rather than reduce, socioeconomic inequality
motions.infoslide: Artificial Intelligence refers to computer systems that perform tasks which would normarlly require human intelligence, and such systems include, but are not limited to, chatbots, image generators, and various advanced automation systems. The rise of Artificial Intelligence refers to the widespread adoption and increasing use of AI technologies across society, such to education, healthcare, business, and entertainment. Socioeconomic (in)equality is normally measured through differences in income, wealth and living standards.

motions.motion: This house prefers a world where the dominant norm for classical novels is experimental adaptations over true-to-novel adaptations
motions.infoslide: For the purpose of this debate, experimental adaptations are those which meaningfully deviate from core elements of the original story when a novel is adapted into a film, play, series, or other form of media. This includes changes to the message, plot, characters, and/or setting. Examples include Wuthering Heights (2026), Clueless (2026), The Bride! (2026), and Apocalypse Now (1979).

motions.motion: This house supports the American Dream as a dominant narrative in society
motions.infoslide: The American Dream is the belief that anyone, regardless of where they were born or what class they were born into, can attain success, since upward mobility is possible for everyone. The American Dream is achieved through sacrifice, risk-taking, and hard work, rather than by chance.

motions.motion: This House would ban personalized advertising on the internet
motions.infoslide: Personalized advertising is when companies track what you do online to choose exactly which ads you see. For example, if you spend the afternoon watching gaming videos or searching for shoes, websites will automatically start showing you ads for those exact items.

motions.motion: This House would ban the use of ghostwriters in the entertainment industry (e.g., for music, celebrity books, and social media).
motions.infoslide: A ghostwriter is a professional writer who is paid to write a book, song, speech, or social media post for someone else. Once the work is finished, the ghostwriter is paid a fee but receives no public credit. Instead, the piece is published under the name of the famous celebrity, politician, musician, or influencer, making it look as though they wrote it themselves.

motions.motion: This House believes that the amount of money in professional sport should be reduced.
motions.infoslide: Professional sport often includes high value transactions like player transfers and sponsorships. There is a lot of investment in infrastructure like stadiums or arenas and large tournaments are accompanied by high ticket prices, purchases of broadcasting rights and ad revenue. Professional athletes of the highest level can earn up to 500 000 USD a week, while large leagues like the NBA are worth around 5 billion USD.

motions.motion: This House believes that movie studios should focus on creating entirely new original characters rather than changing the gender, race, or background of existing legacy characters in remakes.
motions.infoslide: A legacy character is an iconic, deeply established fictional character who has been famous for decades, such as James Bond, Ariel the Little Mermaid, Spider-Man, or Batman. When movie studios want to update an old franchise or make a remake, they sometimes change the race, gender, or background of these legacy characters to make the cast more diverse (for example, casting a Black actress as Ariel). An original character is a brand-new persona created from scratch with their own unique backstory, lore, and identity.

motions.motion: This House supports the rise of self-care culture.
motions.infoslide: Self-care culture refers to the growing emphasis on prioritizing one's own physical, mental, and emotional well-being. This can include healthy habits such as balanced food choices, exercise, sleep, and hygiene, as well as practices like setting personal boundaries, taking breaks from work or school, and focusing on mental health. Critics argue that self-care culture can encourage excessive focus on oneself or turn well-being into a commercial industry, while supporters believe it helps people live healthier and happier lives.

motions.motion: This House would abolish the minimum age requirement to vote, replacing it with a basic political literacy test.
motions.infoslide: Currently, almost every country uses a minimum age requirement (usually 18) to decide who is allowed to vote. A political literacy test is an exam that measures a person’s basic understanding of how their country’s government works.

motions.motion: This House believes that education should prioritize preparing students for employment over general education.
motions.infoslide: Preparation for employment includes training students for more practical vocations and sometimes redirecting them to fields that are more in demand. General education prioritises covering a wide spectrum of different fields some of which may not be connected to the job the students end up working.

motions.motion: This House believes that major tourist destinations should significantly limit the number of tourists that can visit
motions.infoslide: Countries that heavily rely on tourism earn a lot of money from visitors. They often primarily focus on tourism and as a result develop their other industries less. Tourist spots often include historic locations, popular destinations, national parks and many more. To significantly limit the number of tourists means to allow fewer people to visit each destination over a period of time.

motions.motion: This House would ban the commercial media based on real-world criminals.
motions.infoslide: Commercial media based on real-world criminals refers to entertainment made for profit — such as Hollywood movies, television series, and video games that dramatize, re-enact, or tell the life stories of actual, living, or historical criminals.

motions.motion: The Filial piety laws require adult children to financially support impoverished family members or relatives, as opposed to the state providing for their care.
motions.infoslide: The Filial piety laws require adult children to financially support impoverished family members or relatives, as opposed to the state providing for their care.

motions.motion: This House Believes That the environmental movement should prioritize advocating for radical individual lifestyle change over solutions by organizations and/or institutions.
motions.infoslide: Some examples of the changes of lifestyle can be, including but not limited to, vegan diets, limited air travel, recycling, reusing, and composting, etc. Some examples of organizational and/or institutional responsibility can be, including but not limited to, carbon capture technologies, reducing pollution, waste, natural resource consumption, and emissions through its manufacturing process, as applicable, etc.

motions.motion: This House opposes the narrative of identifying sodiers as Heroes.
motions.infoslide: In the status quo, soldiers (usually ones who are part of the armed forces of a country) who fight and at times sacrifice their lives are identified as "heroes" by majority.

motions.motion: TH regrets the trend of celebrities and artists openly endorsing politicians.
motions.infoslide: In the status quo, it has become a common scenario for famous celebrities and artists such as Musicians come in and support candidates, including but not limited to, ones running to be Presidents and/or head of states or Member of Parliament, at times.

motions.motion: TH regrets the commecialization of "self care" products.
motions.infoslide: "Self-care refers to activities that give people time to relax and prioritize their own wellbeing, examples may include but not limited to, spas, massages, meditation, etc. Commercialization refers to the process of actively managing or using something with the intent of gaining financially."

motions.motion: THW mandate that all secondary and post-secondary examinations be open-book tests.
motions.infoslide: Open-book tests refer to assessments or tests, where students are permitted to use their notes and textbooks. Typically, students are still not allowed to access the internet during open-book tests. Secondary and post-secondary would refer to grades 8 and above.

motions.motion: TH would support to have Open borders.
motions.infoslide: In the status quo, there are 195 globally recognized countries in the world; they have their own borders that separates and/or distinguishes them from other countries and/or states. Open borders refers to a country or countries that have no limits on immigration - in other words, people can move freely between countries as they please and would desire.

motions.motion: This House Would ban Moonlighting.
motions.infoslide: The concept of "Moonlighting" is to have a second job and/or work in addition to one's regular employment; there are multiple reasons that can influence a person towards opting for Moonlighting.

motions.motion: This house believes that it is better to have loved and lost than to never have loved at all.
This house opposes the narrative that "music is a universal language".
This house prefers a world where individuals can implement Mute.
motions.infoslide: "Mute" is a technology that can be implemented into one's brain where an individual can decide to mute another entity, ranging from a specific person or group of people. The effect of muting is that the muted entity is no longer seen, heard, or felt by the individual.

motions.motion: This house would abolish anti-social behaviour orders
motions.infoslide: Anti-social behaviour orders are civil penalties applied to people found to be engaging in anti-social behaviour (e.g. drunkenness, swearing, rioting, vandalism, racist language)

motions.motion: This house, as South Korean government, would actiely facilitate the remilitarization of Japan.
motions.infoslide: Japan has recently shifted its security approach from a pacifist stance based on the post-World War II Constitution to an increase in military spending, an expanded military role, and more intensive joint military exercises with the US. The recently elected Prime Minister of Japan, Shigeru Ishiba, is also well known for his advocacy for a more rapid remilitarization.

motions.motion: This house believes that the feminist movement should prioritize supporting positive masculinity over abolishing gender roles
motions.infoslide: Positive Masculinity mantains gender roles for men but attempts to match those roles with positive traits (e.g., empathy, caretaking, social responsibility and self-control)

motions.motion: This house prefers the characterisation of politics as a 'career' instead of 'social/public service'.
This house believes that Australasian countries should disincentivise students from pursuing education in other countries
This house would severely limit companies ability to replace workers with technology
motions.infoslide: In recent years, companies have increasingly replaced workers with technology, primarily through the automation of tasks once performed by humans. Examples include, but are not limited to, replacing factory workers with robotic arms, utilizing artificial intelligence to replace human interaction, and implementing self-service machines.

motions.motion: This house, as an average individual in the world today, would prefer to be immortal
motions.infoslide: The quality of life in the 2000s has improved significantly compared to previous centuries, as evidenced by advancements in technology, education, and healthcare. However, this progress is accompanied by significant challenges, including international conflicts, climate change, and racial tension.

motions.motion: This house believes that governments should strongly prioritise stopping climate change, even if it comes at the cost of economic growth
motions.infoslide: Climate change is the long-term warming of Earth caused by human activities, like burning fossil fuels and deforestation, which release greenhouse gases that trap heat. Addressing climate change can impact economic growth by requiring costly changes to energy systems, stricter regulations on factories, or reduced use of cheap but polluting resources.

motions.motion: This house believes that schools should teach children to question parental authority
motions.infoslide: Parental authority refers to parents' rights and responsibilities toward their children. Because of parental authority, parents make decisions that affect their children's well-being.

motions.motion: This house supports the rise of academic piracy sites (E.g. Sci-Hub, Lib-Gen)
motions.infoslide: Academic piracy refers to unauthorised access and distribution of scientific articles. Websites like Sci-Hub and Lib-Gen provide free access to books and journals that otherwise are restricted to users who pay a fee to access them.

motions.motion: This house regrets the rise of child influencers
motions.infoslide: Child influencer is who has built or is building a presence on social media platforms (TikTok, Instagram, Youtube) creating content to generate views and engagements, that is often sponsored. Examples are Like Natasya, Vlad and Niki, etc.

motions.motion: This house would abolish juries in criminal trials
motions.infoslide: A jury is a group of people brought together during a trial. By listening to the evidence, they decide whether the accused person in a trial is guilty or not guilty. If the jury finds the defendant guilty, the judge then decides the appropriate punishment.

motions.motion: This house believes that all international sporting events (e.g. FIFA World Cup, the Olympic Games) should have a permanent host nation
motions.infoslide: A host nation provides the facilities for that event to take place. Currently, the host of an international sporting event is decided per edition. For example, Tokyo hosted the 2020 Olympics while Paris hosted the 2024 Olympics because these cities were supported by their country in bidding to host the Games.

motions.motion: This house prefers the depiction of platonic over romantic relationships in media
motions.infoslide: A platonic relationship is a close bond between people that does not involve romantic elements. This contrasts with a romantic relationship, which typically includes emotional closeness and feelings of romance or love.

motions.motion: This house would invest in long-term debate classes for their child
motions.infoslide: You are the parent of a young child currently in middle school who has never displayed interest in debate before. You would like your child to become a good debater as you have become aware that it is very helpful for university applications and general success.

motions.motion: This house regrets the rise of thrifting
motions.infoslide: Thrifting refers to the practice of buying second-hand clothing and other items, often from charity shops, vintage stores, or online resale platforms. It has grown significantly in popularity in recent years, particularly among younger consumers

motions.motion: This house would remove patents for eco-friendly technologies.
motions.infoslide: Patents give inventors exclusive rights to produce or sell their inventions for a certain period of time, preventing others from copying or using the same technology without permission

motions.motion: This house believes that religious leaders should not endorse candidates for political office.
motions.infoslide: Religious leaders are people who guide others in their faith, like priests, imams, rabbis, or monks. Political office means a job in the government where someone makes laws or important decisions for the public, like being a mayor, president, or member of parliament.

motions.motion: This house believes that history education should prioritize the experiences of everyday people over the actions of great leaders.
motions.infoslide: Great leaders are famous people from the past, like kings, generals, or presidents, who had a big influence on their country or the world, for example Napoleon or Martin Luther King Jr.

motions.motion: Space exploration means sending people, rockets, or machines into outer space to learn more about planets, stars, and the universe. It includes things like landing on the Moon, exploring Mars, or using telescopes and satellites to study space.
motions.infoslide: This house supports the rise of space exploration

motions.motion: This house would invest into the development of AI caretakers for children and old people
motions.infoslide: AI caretakers refer to artificially intelligent systems or robots designed to help care for vulnerable groups, such as children or the elderly. These technologies may assist with tasks like supervision, companionship, health monitoring, education, or daily routines.

motions.motion: This house prefers a world where individuals are aware of their “natural expiration date”
motions.infoslide: A ‘natural expiration date’ refers to the date whereby an individual would die. This date is determined based on the physical and mental condition of the individual when they are 18.

motions.motion: Many schools use grades (like A–F or 1–5) to show how well a student is doing. Written feedback means the teacher explains in words what the student did well and what they need to improve, instead of giving a number or letter.
motions.infoslide: This house would ban grades and replace them with written feedback.

motions.motion: This house believes that rich countries should do more to fight climate change than poor countries.
motions.infoslide: Rich countries (also called developed countries) are nations with strong economies, advanced technology, and higher living standards (e.g., USA, Germany, Japan). Poor countries (developing countries) have weaker economies, less technology, and lower living standards (e.g., Bangladesh, Kenya, Nepal). Climate change means long-term changes in the Earth’s climate caused mainly by greenhouse gases from human activity. Examples of its effects include rising sea levels, stronger storms, droughts, and floods.

motions.motion: This house would ban beauty filters on social media.
motions.infoslide: "Beauty filters” are special effects on apps like Instagram, TikTok, or Snapchat that change how someone looks. For example, by making their skin smoother, eyes bigger, or face slimmer. Banning beauty filters means people would not be allowed to use or post pictures and videos with these effects.

motions.motion: This house believes that the final say in the medical treatment of minors should belong to medical professionals (doctors) instead of parents
motions.infoslide: A minor means someone under 18 years old (a child or teenager who is not yet an adult). In many countries, parents normally make the final decision about their child’s medical treatment (like surgery or medicine)

motions.motion: This house believes that childhood years are the best years of life.
motions.infoslide: Childhood usually means the years before adulthood, when people are still dependent on their parents or guardians (commonly before the age of 18).

motions.motion: This house would make museums free for everyone.
motions.infoslide: Museums are places where people can see and learn about history, art, science, and culture. For example, old artifacts, famous paintings, or dinosaur fossils. Right now, many museums charge an entry fee. Making museums free means anyone can visit them without paying any money.

motions.motion: This house would rather live in a world where God sometimes does wrong things than in a world where God is always right.
motions.infoslide: In this debate, “a world where God sometimes does wrong things” means a world where God can make mistakes or act unfairly, just like humans sometimes do. “A world where God is always right” means God never makes mistakes and everything He does is always good and perfect.

motions.motion: This house would ban companies from making a profit from life-saving drugs.
motions.infoslide: “Life-saving drugs” are medicines that people need to survive. For example, insulin for diabetes, or medicine for cancer or serious infections.

motions.motion: This house believes helping poor people in your own country is more important than helping people overseas.
motions.infoslide: Helping people in your own country means giving food, money, homes, or jobs to citizens who are struggling. Helping people overseas means sending aid, donations, or supplies to other countries, especially during disasters or wars.

motions.motion: In times of economic need, THBT states should sell and/or mortgage their national treasures and monuments
THBT the human body should be government-owned property upon death
THW actively encourage families to have only one child
THW ban all fast food advertisements.
motions.infoslide: Fast food are easily prepared processed food served in snack bars and restaurants as a quick meal. Some of the fast food establishments in South Korea include, Lotteria, McDonald's, Mom's Touch and Kyochon Chicken.

motions.motion: THW allow South Koreans to volunteer and fight in foreign legions
motions.infoslide: Foreign legions are volunteer military units made up of international fighters. Most of these foreign soldiers are former servicemen with military experience. Examples of foreign legions include, but are not limited to, the Kurdish People's Protection Unit in Syria, Peshmerga in Iraq, and the most recent International Legion of Territorial Defense of Ukraine. In light of the recent Russian invasion of Ukraine, several South Koreans including South Korean former Navy Seal and YouTuber Ken Rhee has gone to Ukraine to fight the Russians. The Ministry of Foreign Affairs issued a warning in early March that citizens who enter Ukraine without permission are subject to criminal punishment and administrative restrictions.

motions.motion: THBT welfare for the poor should be given in cash, without condition
motions.infoslide: Welfare policies for the poor usually provide assistance in the form of vouchers, food stamps and subsidies where recipients are meant to spend them on specific items, usually essentials. The recipients are directed to use these supports to buy subsidised grains, apartment rental, healthcare or daily items such as kerosene. In certain cash-transfer welfare policies, welfare recipients are given cash. They must however fulfill certain conditions such as vaccinating their children, keeping their children in school and/or making regular health visits. Unconditional transfers are those that are given to beneficiaries without any specific requirements beyond income eligibility.

motions.motion: THBT South Korea should impose a Casino Entry Levy and allow its citizens to gamble at all local casinos.
motions.infoslide: Lotteries, horse racing, boat racing, and cycling are all legal forms of gambling in South Korea. However, casino gambling is strictly prohibited. South Koreans face a three-year prison sentence if they’re caught at a casino. Any citizen found to have gambled in a casino abroad can also face prosecution on their return to the country. There are approximately 23 land-based casinos in the South Korea that are meant for tourists to gamble at. Only the Kangwon Land Casino, located near Pyeongchang, allows Koreans to gamble. --- Singapore Citizens who wish to gamble in Singapore casinos must pay for a Casino Entry Levy prior to entering the casino premises. These entry levies allow patrons to enter the casino premises for a fixed period of time. A day-levy (24 hours) costs USD120, and an annual levy (365 days) costs ~USD2400. It is an offence for Singapore Citizens to enter the casino without a valid entry levy ticket. Tourists do not need to purchase a Casino Entry Levy.

motions.motion: THW ban schools from giving homework to students.
motions.infoslide: Homework, or a homework assignment, is a set of tasks assigned to students by their teachers to be completed outside the classroom and school hours.

motions.motion: THW allow victims to have a say in the punishments meted out to school bullies.
motions.infoslide: Bullying in school can exist in many forms: It can be physical (pushing, punching, or hitting); verbal (name-calling or threats); or psychological and emotional (spreading rumors or excluding someone from a conversation or activity). With the pervasive use of social media, bullying can occur outside of school hours via emails, text messages, and Facebook posts. These exchanges are known as cyberbullying.

motions.motion: Jane robbed a bank at gun point. In her bid to escape the police, she fell and hit her head. As a result of the fall, she lost all her memories after age 5. THW not convict Jane of any crime.
motions.infoslide: When someone is found and proven guilty of committing a crime, they are convicted of that crime and must serve the penalty and punishment as required by the law.

motions.motion: THBT communications are better on social media
motions.infoslide: Social media is an internet-based form of communication that allows users to converse and interact with each other virtually.

motions.motion: THB video gaming should be included in school curriculum
THBT all new public transportation contracts should only be awarded to fully electric bids
motions.infoslide: Public transit is a system of vehicles used to transport a mass of people from one place to another such as trains and buses. An electric bid means that a public transportation vehicle uses electricity as its primary source of energy generation in order to function.

motions.motion: THBT teachers should be replaced by computers
motions.infoslide: A computer is a machine or device that can be instructed to perform processes, calculations, and other operations. With the improvement of technology, computers have evolved from only being able to perform simple arithmetic tasks to being capable of carrying out complex functions.

motions.motion: THBT school students are too young for romance
THW set up homeless shelters in wealthy neighborhoods
motions.infoslide: Due to poverty, many people often find themselves in a situation of homelessness, which is the state of not having stable, safe, and adequate housing. Government and non-government agencies usually build homeless shelters that provide temporary residence for homeless individuals and families.

motions.motion: THS the rise of children social media influencers
motions.infoslide: Social media is an internet-based form of communication that allows users to create web content and interact with each other. People who have large audiences of followers on their social media accounts are called social media influencers.

motions.motion: THW ban plastic bags

THBT countries with a track record of human rights violations (e.g. Qatar, Russia, etc.) should be banned from hosting major sporting events and the citizens of those countries should be disallowed from owning sports teams.
motions.infoslide: Countries with authoritarian political structures are generally understood to have a track record of human rights violations through taking away rights of people. Example of such countries are Qatar, Russia, Saudi Arabia, etc. In recent times, there has been a trend of such countries hosting major sporting events and institutions of such countries owning sports teams.

motions.motion: TH prefers a world where art cannot be generated by artificial intelligence and would always require significant human input
motions.infoslide: Artificial Intelligence is the simulation of human intelligence processes by machines, especially computer systems that can perform a lot of activities including generation of artwork

motions.motion: TH would impose term limits on head of state positions in democracies
motions.infoslide: In democracies head of states generally have 3 to 5 years terms. A term limit refers to a maximum number of term limits that a head of states can have e.g.: a US president can be elected a maximum of 2 terms

motions.motion: This House prefers a world where people do not feel anger
motions.infoslide: for the purpose of this debate, people are able to experience a world without feeling a particular emotion

motions.motion: This House would give more votes to low income individuals
motions.infoslide: for the purposes of this debate, the number of votes someone can have in an election can exceed the normal one vote per person

motions.motion: This House Believes That western liberal democracies should penalize tourism in countries that abuse the human rights of their citizens
motions.infoslide: western liberal democracies refer to countries from the global west along with Australia and New Zealand that generally have open and democratic countries.

motions.motion: This House Would allow ex-convicts to join the police force
motions.infoslide: an ex-convict is an individual who has been deemed guilty of a crime, punished, and has fully served their punishment. For example, someone that has finished the entirety of their prison sentence and is now allowed to go back into society is an ex-convict.

motions.motion: This House Would tax non-renewable energy to match the cost of the cheapest available renewable energy source
motions.infoslide: Currently renewable energy sources cost more per unit of energy than non-renewable sources of energy. The two prices can only be matched through a tax imposed on non-renewable sources such that it rises up to match renewable energy sources.

motions.motion: This House believes that primary and secondary schools should allocate an equal portion of time in school to academic and extracurricular activities
motions.infoslide: extracurricular activities refer to activities that are beyond the academic curriculum of children such as debating, sports, etc.

motions.motion: TH, as young professional in China, would support the "lying flat" (tangping) movement
THBT universities should implement race-blind affirmative action admissions
THW break up major media conglomerates (e.g. Disney)
THP a world in which individuals prioritized the pursuit of their own happiness over their families
THW mandate that buy-outs of major social media platforms be subject to a vote of approval by their users
THBT the Bretton Woods Institutions have done more harm than good
TH, as parent from a minority community, Would raise their child to have pessimistic outlook on life
TH, as the Republican Party, would abandon the strategy of claiming the 2020 presidential elections were fraudulent
THBT States should aggressively prioritize funding rent-assistance programs over public housing project
TH Regrets the narrative that children are inherently pure and good
motions.infoslide: Currently, children are believed to be inherently good and pure beings. Meaning, even if they can be influenced by bad actors and actions, they start out innocent and good and can go back to being good since this is a built-in characteristic in them.

motions.motion: THR the rise of influencer-lead marketing campaigns
This House Would pay stay-at-home mothers a salary
motions.infoslide: A stay-at-home mother is a mother who chooses not to seek formal employment and chooses to raise their children and manage the household.

motions.motion: TH Would allow employers to access, or request access to, the criminal records of job applicants
motions.infoslide: Currently, employers are allowed to access the criminal record of job applicants. This gives them access to knowledge of if an applicant has ever been convicted of a crime or imprisoned.

motions.motion: TH prefers a world without any government restrictions on international migration
motions.infoslide: Currently, governments use passport applications, visa applications, and immigration restrictions to control and limit the people moving into and out of countries.

motions.motion: TH welcomes the automation of labor
motions.infoslide: The automation of labor is the trend of substituting technology for human labor to perform tasks and jobs that technology can reasonably carry out.

motions.motion: THP a world without a conception of the afterlife
motions.infoslide: The afterlife is a concept in philosophy and religion that claims that individuals carry on living in one form or another after death. e.g Reincarnation, Heaven, and Hell.

motions.motion: THW revoke all government subsidized health benefits for individuals who choose to lead unhealthy lifestyles
motions.infoslide: An unhealthy lifestyle is a way of living that results in long term harms to one's health. E.g smoking, eatings fast food, and drinking excessive volumes of alcohol.

motions.motion: This House Believes That internet anonymity does more harm than good
motions.infoslide: Internet anonymity is the practice of not using one's real name and identity when interacting on the internet.

motions.motion: This house believes that feminist movements in developing nations should publicly disavow western forms of feminism
motions.infoslide: Western feminism refers to the feminist movements in the Western world, as well as their particular goals and strategies (e.g. closing the gender pay gap, using high profile celebrities as spokespeople)

motions.motion: This house believes that governments should be able to censor the publication of economic news during confidence crises (e.g. stock market crashes, bank runs)
motions.infoslide: Confidence crises occur in economies when there is a general belief that the economy is doing poorly. This can often prompt rapid selling of shares, or bank runs when people seek to withdraw their money from banks because they fear that they could collapse. This can form a feedback loop that worsens the crisis by tanking the stock market or causing a credit crisis.

motions.motion: This house opposes China's effort to deepen its relationship with Myanmar
motions.infoslide: The governments of China and Myanmar have grown closer since the military coup in Myanmar in 2021. The two foreign ministers of each country have agreed to 1) establish a China-Myanmar Economic Corridor, 2) boost a range of cross-border cooperation including cross-border industrial zones, 3) increase Chinese support for development in Myanmar 4) allow the Myanmar regime to open a consulate in Chongqing, and 5) that China will provide a 650 million RMB grant to Myanmar, among other measures. The Myanmar junta has also agreed to open a Liquid Natural Gas plant financed by China, and is likely to approve multiple other new Chinese projects within the Economic Corridor. The Myanmar government has recently ramped up its war against rebel groups, and has been documented to have repeatedly committed atrocities against civilians.

motions.motion: This house would ban sports with a significant risk of traumatic head injury
motions.infoslide: Several popular sports (e.g. boxing, rugby) have relatively high risk of traumatic head injuries, which can cause significant damage to the brain. In response to several high-profile cases of brain damage in former athletes in later life, some of these sports have recently updated their concussion protocols to try to reduce the risk to athletes (e.g. making it easier for doctors to force a boxing match to end for medical reasons)

motions.motion: This house believes that the state should offer criminals the option of a 'new identity' upon completing a prison sentence.
motions.infoslide: A 'new identity' for the purposes of this debate includes a new name, relocation to a new area, a new background story, and optional plastic surgery.

motions.motion: This house would ban private tutoring
motions.infoslide: Private tutoring is a type of teaching that occurs outside the formal government education system. It is usually sought after by parents who wish for their children to excel in school. It is not subject to any curriculum requirements, and frequently has limited government oversight. Some examples include 'cram schools' in many Asian countries, as well as one-on-one private lessons in particular subjects (e.g. Maths). Private tutoring can consist of one on one lessons or group lessons.

motions.motion: This house would reduce the minimum qualifications required to become a teacher
motions.infoslide: Currently, many countries require school teachers to have a university degree in the subject they are teaching, as well as secondary degree specifically in teaching. Most countries in the world are experiencing a teacher shortage due to relatively low wage, high entry requirements and poor working condition.

motions.motion: That we should focus on societal factors (e.g. socioeconomic factors) as opposed to individual factors (e.g. personal history) when addressing the causes and consequences of mental health issues
motions.infoslide: Socioeconomic factors refer to the social and economic conditions that shape an individual's life, including their income, education, employment, social status, and access to resources and services. Individual factors, on the other hand, refer to an individual's personal characteristics and experiences, including their genetics, personality, life history, and coping strategies.

motions.motion: TH supports the development and use of moral enhancement drugs
That medical staff should be able to opt out of providing treatments on religious or ethical grounds.
motions.infoslide: Medical staff may opt out of providing treatments on religious or ethical grounds, such as objections to procedures like abortions or medications that conflict with their beliefs. For example, some may object to certain procedures such as abortions or assisted suicide, on the basis of religious teachings or ethical principles that prohibit taking a human life.

motions.motion: That we should ban juvenile detention centers, and, instead, force all children who commit crimes to complete community service
motions.infoslide: judge will determine the appropriate amount of community service a child needs to perform. This debate should not be about the exact amount of community sentence that would be appropriate.

motions.motion: THW establish a universal passport
motions.infoslide: a universal passport refers to a single document or system that can be used to verify an individual's identity and citizenship status across multiple countries. This would mean that individuals would not need to obtain different documents, such as visas or travel permits, for each country they want to visit or reside in.

motions.motion: THBT individuals with bad personal lifestyles (that influence their health) should pay more for their health insurance
motions.infoslide: bad personal lifestyles refer to habits and behaviors that can engatively impact an individual's health. Examples include smoking, excesseive alcohol consumption, poor diet, and drug abuse.

motions.motion: That adult children, where they are financially capable, should have a legal obligation to look after their elderly parents
motions.infoslide: for the purpose of this debate, this law might force adult children to pay for their parents' food, clothing, shelter and medical needs. If children fail to do so, they can be fined, or even go to jail.

motions.motion: This house supports the creation of an AI non-proliferation treaty
motions.infoslide: An AI non-proliferation treaty would involve governments mutually agreeing to cease developing artificial intelligence for military and espionage purposes

motions.motion: This house prefers Disney optimism to Ghibli optimism
This house believes that humanity should invest considerable amount of resources into the exploration of deep space
This house would require all politicians to use publicly run utilities/infrastructure where the options exists to do so
motions.infoslide: Publicly run utilities/infrastructure are services provided by the government, for example public schools, public transportation and government-run hospitals.

motions.motion: This house regrets the belief that journalists should cover 'both sides' of current affairs
motions.infoslide: There is a norm in media that journalisms should provide a platform to all sides of contentious issues, for instance by interviewing supporters and opponents of a policy, and treating different perspectives on contentious issues as valid and worthy of receiving coverage.

motions.motion: This house regrets the rise of teenagers at the forefront of social movements
motions.infoslide: In recent years, teenagers and young people (e.g. Greta Thunberg, Malala Yousafzai, the Parkland students) have increasingly been central parts of campaigns by social movements (e.g. the climate movement, feminism, and in favour of gun control). This means they have advocated publicly for their positive, received extension media attention, and campaigned directly to politicians.

motions.motion: This house supports the active integration of ChatGPT (and technology of similar functionalities) as a tool for education in schools (secondary schools onwards)
motions.infoslide: ChatGPT is a powerful artificial intelligence (AI) system that uses advanced neural networks to understand and generate human-like text, learning from an enormous amount of data to provide helpful and accurate responses to your questions or prompts

motions.motion: This house believes that developing countries should heavily discourage urbanization
motions.infoslide: Urbanization is the process where more and more people move from rural areas (like the countryside or small villages) to urban areas (like cities and towns). As cities grow, they attract even more people, creating a cycle of expansion

motions.motion: This house believes that, in established democracies, all major government policy decisions should be decided through referendum.
motions.infoslide: A referendum is a direct vote in which the entire electorate is invited to either accept or reject a particular proposal, law, or constitutional amendment. It is a form of direct democracy where citizens have the opportunity to express their opinion on a specific issue by casting their votes.
motions.round: U15 Grand Finals, U14 Grand Finals, U13 Grand Finals

motions.motion: This house regrets the glorification of the criminal way of life in certain series.
motions.infoslide: Narcos, Breaking Bad and Fariña are some examples of series where the central character(s) live a life of crime and various aspects of their ways of going about are portrayed to entertain the audience.
motions.round: U18 Grand Final, U16 Grand Finals, U15 Semifinals, U14 Semifinals, U13 Semifinals

motions.motion: This house prefers a world where gods are seen as imperfect beings.
motions.infoslide: Currently, most religions conceive God as perfect beings which means viewing them as entities that know everything, see everything, and have power over everything in the world.

motions.motion: This house supports the active promotion of Slum Tourism.
motions.infoslide: Slum tourism is a type of tourism that involves visiting impoverished areas - usually through a service provided by tourism agencies. Slum tourism is now becoming increasingly prominent in developing nations, including but not limited to India, Brazil, Kenya and Indonesia.

motions.motion: This house, as a young and emerging artist, would sign up for a music company with Western label model (e.g. Sony Music, Universal Music Group) as opposed to one with a K-pop label model (e.g. HYBE, YG Entertainment)
motions.infoslide: K-pop labels are involved in the training of artists, curation and production of the artist's content, and in creating a steady stream of 'trainee' artists who debut after spending time training with the label. Western labels, on the other hand, often select artists who have reached small amounts of fame, and allow for a lot more creative control over the content they produce.

motions.motion: This house believes that the rise of Startup culture does more harm than good.
motions.infoslide: Startup culture refers to the set of values, norms, practices, and behaviors commonly associated with entrepreneurial ventures and early-stage companies. It encompasses the unique atmosphere, attitudes, and working environment prevalent in startups, characterized by innovation, risk-taking, agility, and a strong emphasis on growth.

motions.motion: This house believes that parents should use fear as a way to instill values in their children.
motions.infoslide: The use of fear to instill values refers to the practice of employing threats or intimidation as a means to influence individuals' behavior and beliefs in alignment with certain moral or ethical principles. This approach relies on creating a sense of apprehension or anxiety about the potential consequences of not adhering to these values.

motions.motion: THBT in countries where men are conscripted, eg: Singapore, South Korea, the feminist movement should advocate for the conscription of women as well
motions.infoslide: Conscription refers to a compulsory period of military service. For example, all men in Switzerland must serve 21 weeks in the military before the age of 25.

motions.motion: THR the commercialisation of sports
motions.infoslide: Commercialisation of sports refers to making profit from sports. For example, by charging lots of money for tickets, or having advertisting during games.
motions.round: U13 Pre-Quarterfinals, U12 Octofinals, U11 Semifinals

motions.motion: THBT governments should aggressively fund space exploration and technologies.
motions.infoslide: Many private companies are developing space technology, for example rockets to go into space and explore other planets.

motions.motion: THBT the government should move citizens and their homes away from areas with very high natural disaster risk.
motions.infoslide: Some areas have a very high risk of natural disasters, for example cities next to volcanoes, or with lots of earthquakes.

motions.motion: THW set the cost of fines based on the criminal's income, rather than the severity of the crime.
motions.infoslide: The punishment for some crimes is paying money (a fine) to the government, rather than going to jail.

motions.motion: THBT school students should be taught how to use AI.
motions.infoslide: AI (artificial intelligence) is computer software with human-like intelligence. It can write, and make images and videos.

motions.motion: THW ban the advertising of unhealthy products.
motions.infoslide: Many companies advertise unhealthy products, such as cigarettes or junk food.

motions.motion: This house would allocate the majority of government sports funding towards fostering participation in lesser-known or underrepresented sports rather than prioritizing investment in mainstream sports within the nation.
motions.infoslide: Mainstream sports are widely popular and played or followed by large numbers of people globally. Examples include soccer, basketball, tennis, football, and baseball. This is in contrast to less well-known sports which refers to sports with a smaller following or limited regional popularity. Examples include handball, curling, lacrosse, sepak takraw and squash.

motions.motion: This house believes that directors from minority communities in major Hollywood studios should impose preferential treatment (prioritizing them in hiring and lead roles, increasing the number studio staff, etc) for the people from their own community in their blockbuster films
motions.infoslide: Preferential treatment refers to the act of giving someone priority or providing them with advantages, benefits, or more favorable conditions over others, often based on specific characteristics such as race, gender, nationality, or socioeconomic status.

motions.motion: This house would allow communities to veto resource mining projects in their local areas.
motions.infoslide: Resource mining is the process of extracting useful materials from the earth. Some examples of substances that are mined include coal, gold, or iron ore.

motions.motion: This house supports the environmental movement's shift towards climate litigation
motions.infoslide: Climate litigation refers to the use of legal action, either through courts or tribunals, to address issues related to climate change. It involves individuals, communities, NGOs, or even governments suing corporations, states, or institutions for their role in contributing to or failing to mitigate climate change.

motions.motion: Assuming the technological feasibility, this house would replace human soldiers in military with Autonomous Robot
motions.infoslide: Autonomous robot is a machine that can perform tasks or make decisions without human intervention, using sensors, algorithms, and artificial intelligence to navigate and adapt to its environment.

motions.motion: In districts where it is dominated with Arabic community, this house believes that Arabic should be the primary language of instruction in schools instead of French.
motions.infoslide: In the 1950s–70s, France encouraged immigration to fill low-wage jobs in industry, construction, and public services. As a result, immigrants from Algeria, Morocco, and Tunisia has come and settled in urban industrial areas such as Paris, Lyon, Marseille, Lille, forming their own community. In these areas, Arabic is frequently spoken at home and in the community.

motions.motion: This house would require startups and new businesses to pass financial sustainability test before qualifying for state subsidies and support
motions.infoslide: A financial sustainability test is a tool or assessment used to evaluate whether a project, organization, or policy can maintain its financial operations over the long term.The test includes but not limited to minimum level of annual nett revenue, continuous operations plan for 2 years, minimum level of ratio of income to operational cost, etc.

motions.motion: As an average person, in Western Liberal Democracies, this house would take the pill.
motions.infoslide: There is a pill that maximizes an individual’s potential based on their aptitude. For example, if someone has the potential to become the world’s greatest chef, the pill will immediately unlock that potential.

motions.motion: That the government should ban all personal mobile devices that can access the internet for people under 18
motions.infoslide: A personal mobile device is any personal device that someone can carry with them, such as (but not limited to) a mobile phone, tablet, or laptop.

motions.motion: This house would abolish sports involving animals.
motions.infoslide: Animal sports involve competitions where animals participate in races, fights, or performances, often showcasing their speed, strength, or skill. Common examples include horse racing, greyhound racing, and bullfighting.

motions.motion: This house would abolish school uniforms
motions.infoslide: A school uniform is a designated attire that all students must wear during school hours. While some schools allow accessories, they must not disrupt the overall attire in any way.

motions.motion: This house opposes the narrative that wisdom increases with age
motions.infoslide: Wisdom is the trait of having good judgement and knowledge, often used in solving problems.

motions.motion: This house regrets the creation of child influencers
motions.infoslide: With the rise of social media, more parents have become influencers, frequently sharing content featuring their children. Many do this to boost their online presence, often posting videos that gain significant attention. Some of these videos go viral, exposing these children to millions of viewers across the internet.

motions.motion: This house would heavily regulate junk entertainment
motions.infoslide: Junk entertainment refers to media that prioritizes shock value, drama, or mindless consumption over substance

motions.motion: This house believes that government should not provide healthcare services to those who lead an unhealthy life.
motions.infoslide: An unhealthy lifestyle includes poor diet, lack of exercise, and harmful habits like smoking or excessive alcohol consumption. This often leads to serious health issues such as obesity and heart disease.

motions.motion: This House opposes the narrative that fear is a flaw.
motions.infoslide: In popular media, fear is often portrayed as a flaw or obstacle that needs to be overcome; however, others argue that fear is a natural and often useful emotion.

motions.motion: This House believes that schools should employ edutainment as their primary education method.
motions.infoslide: Edutainment is a way of learning that combines education with entertainment. It means teaching subjects through fun and engaging methods like games, videos, storytelling, or interactive activities. The goal is to make learning enjoyable rather than relying on activities that require high levels of concentration or effort.

motions.motion: This House prefers a world where there is a one, single global government.
motions.infoslide: A “global government” means a single political system that makes and enforces laws for all countries and people in the world, instead of separate national governments like those of South Korea or the United States.

motions.motion: This House supports resurrection biology.
motions.infoslide: Resurrection biology is the idea of bringing extinct animals back to life using modern science. Scientists use tools like genetic engineering and cloning to recreate species that no longer exist by studying their DNA and combining it with living organisms.

motions.motion: This House prefers to raise their children with Type A personality traits.
motions.infoslide: In a society where individuals can be distinctly categorized into two personality types, Type A individuals are ambitious, organized, proactive, and high-achieving; they tend to set high expectations for themselves. Meanwhile, Type B individuals are easygoing, creative, and relaxed; they are social, less stressed, and tend to “go with the flow".

motions.motion: This House opposes the creation of AI machines for social interactions.
motions.infoslide: As AI technology advances there is a question of whether or not AI should be treated like people in society this includes designing machines that could act as friends or companions.

motions.motion: This House would legalize commercial organ selling.
motions.infoslide: Commercial organ selling refers to the legal buying and selling of human organs (such as kidneys or liver segments) for transplantation, typically involving financial compensation for the donor.

motions.motion: This House, as the South Korean Ministry of Culture, supports the adaptation of South Korean entertainment to appeal to global audiences.
motions.infoslide: The global popularity of South Korean culture refers to how entertainment from South Korea, such as K-pop, movies, and TV shows, has become widely enjoyed around the world. This trend is often called the Hallyu or “Korean Wave.” Popular works like Squid Game and Parasite have helped shape how people in other countries see South Korea, increasing interest in its culture and boosting tourism. At the same time, some argue that South Korean entertainment has adapted its style to appeal more to global (especially Western) audiences.

motions.motion: THP a world where a god (or gods) is viewed as dystheistic rather than morally infallable
motions.infoslide: Dystheism is a theological concept that refers to the belief in a deity or deities who are not wholly good or benevolent, and may even be considered malevolent or evil. Significant examples of religions that exhibit a dystheistic perspective include the belief system of the Ancient Greeks, who worshipped gods that frequently engaged in conflicts among themselves and manipulated humans for their own entertainment. Another example is Hinduism, where one of the gods, Shiva, is directly associated with deliberate destruction directed at people. The term ""morally infallible"" refers to the idea that an entity, typically a deity or a divine being, is incapable of making any moral mistakes or committing any morally wrong actions. In other words, a morally infallible being is perfect in its moral judgment and behavior, always acting in alignment with moral principles and never engaging in morally objectionable acts.

motions.motion: THW require companies to report all power harassment complaints to the Labour Standards Bureau for investigation and prosecution instead of settling these grievances internally.
motions.infoslide: Workplace bullying, (also known as “Power harassment” in Japan) is defined as a form of bullying by those who are in a higher work position. Based on Japan's Labor Policy Comprehensive Promotion Act, Power Harassment consists of “remarks and behaviour of people taking advantage of their superior positions in the workplace that exceed what is necessary and appropriate for the conduct of business, thereby harming the working environment of employees”. Examples of Power Harassment include, but are not limited to, physical abuse, emotional abuse (e.g. threats, defamation, insults, rants), and excessive demand at work (e.g. unnecessary requests, unfulfillable tasks, obstruction of work).

motions.motion: THW hold internet service providers and website companies legally liable for most content created by users of their services, including content that could be considered false or defamatory
motions.infoslide: in many jurisdictions, online platforms are largely granted immunity from civil liability (such as fines) for content posted by others on their platforms, as well as for removing or failing to remove said content with the exception of copyright claims

motions.motion: This House believes that Japan should seek to address its aging population crisis by focusing on opening its border to more immigrants, investing in automation, and encourage longer working lives as opposed to encouraging a higher birth rate (e.g. subsidies for child care, baby bonuses, paternity leave)
motions.infoslide: Japan is facing an aging population crisis, characterized by a rapidly increasing proportion of elderly citizens, a declining irth rate, and a shrinking workforce. This demographic shift is a result of several factors, including increasing life expectancy and low fertility rates.

motions.motion: This House regrets the decline of lifetime employment offers
motions.infoslide: Shūshin koyō (終身雇用) is the term for permanent employment in Japan that evolved from Japan’s impressive postwar economic growth during the Showa Era. In the practice of lifetime employment, companies typically hire new college graduates and implicitly guarantee them stable employment until they reach the retirement age. This gave rise to the notion of a salaryman with unshakable loyalty to their company in exchange for lifetime employment security. Japan's lifetime employment system, backed by seniority-based wages, became the standard after the 1950's. Today many believe that the concept of lifetime employment is outdated. In fact, only 8.8 percent of Japanese companies now have lifetime employment policies in place

motions.motion: THBT convicted members of the Yakuza should not have the right to vote.
motions.infoslide: The Yakuza is a collective term for Japanese organized crime syndicates, similar to the Mafia in Italy or other organized crime groups around the world. Yakuza groups are involved in a wide range of illegal activities, including drug trafficking, illegal gambling, loan sharking, extortion, human trafficking, and various white-collar crimes. They are also known to have legitimate business interests, which they use to launder money and maintain influence in society. At its peak in the 1960s, the yakuza had more than 180,000 members. Of late, stricter laws such as those forbidding yakuza members from opening bank accounts, obtaining a credit card, taking out insurance policies or even signing a contract for a mobile phone have made a life of crime increasingly unappealing. There are thought to be about 25,000 active yakuza members in Japan today.

motions.motion: TH supports the move to restart or build new nuclear power plants in Japan.
motions.infoslide: Nuclear energy has been a national strategic priority for Japan since 1973. Japan's reactors provided some 30% of the country's electricity before the Fukushima accident in 2011. The Fukushima accident was a nuclear disaster that occurred at the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant in Fukushima Prefecture, Japan. It was triggered by a massive 9.0-magnitude earthquake and subsequent tsunami, which caused widespread destruction along the northeastern coast of Japan. Radioactive particles were carried by wind and rain leading to widespread radioactive contamination of land, air, and water in the surrounding areas. The disaster also prompted the evacuation of over 150,000 people from the area surrounding the power plant, creating a large exclusion zone. The cleanup and decommissioning process is estimated to take several decades to complete. Since then, the government has reduced the role of nuclear power. In early 2023, Japan's Cabinet approved plans to extend the lifespans of nuclear reactors beyond 60 years and build new units to replace those that are decommissioned, reversing policies put in place after the Fukushima disaster in 2011.

motions.motion: TH supports Anonymous in its retaliation against injustice where the state seems unwilling or unable to do so
motions.infoslide: Hacktivism refers to the use of computer hacking skills, cyber techniques, and digital tools to promote, advance, or protest a social, political, or environmental issue. Hacktivists, or individuals who engage in hacktivism, often employ tactics such as website defacement, distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks, data breaches, or the release of sensitive or confidential information to make a statement or raise awareness about a cause. Anonymous, a group of hacktivist, employs coordinated cyberattacks against various world governments, corporations or other groups, often in the name of social or political causes. On February 25, 2022, Anonymous declared that they had launched 'cyber operations' against the Russian Federation, in retaliation for the invasion of Ukraine ordered by Russian president Vladimir Putin. Since then, the group has claimed responsibility for various cyberattacks that disabled websites and leaked data from Russian government agencies

motions.motion: TH, as Chie Yasutake, would keep the child
motions.infoslide: Hana's Miso Soup was an iconic Japanese movie about a young woman, Chie Yasutake. In the movie, Chie, who is a recent college graduate, becomes engaged to the love of her life, Shingo. Her life, however, takes an unexpected turn when she is diagnosed with terminal cancer.
